claims.claim_text: 70% of Week 1 bugs are really environment issues.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? The Hidden Cost of Bad Setup
claims.anchor_text: **70% of "bugs"** in Week 1 are actually environment issues, not code bugs
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Many early "bugs"** in Week 1 turn out to be environment issues rather than code bugs
claims.rationale: This is an internal-seeming statistic with no attributable source or reproducible dataset.

claims.claim_text: Students lose 8-12 hours debugging setup problems.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? The Hidden Cost of Bad Setup
claims.anchor_text: **Average time lost**: 8-12 hours debugging setup problems
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Average time lost**: setup problems can easily consume hours of debugging time
claims.rationale: The precise range is not sourced and reads as fabricated precision.

claims.claim_text: Tooling frustration is the number-one reason students quit before learning starts.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? The Hidden Cost of Bad Setup
claims.anchor_text: **#1 reason students quit**: Frustration with tooling before they even start learning
claims.suggested_rewrite: **A common reason students quit early**: frustration with tooling before they get to the actual learning
claims.rationale: A ranked causal claim needs real survey data or retention analysis.

claims.claim_text: The term dependency hell was coined in the late 1990s.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? The Hidden Cost of Bad Setup
claims.anchor_text: The term "dependency hell" was coined in the late 1990s to describe the nightmare of managing software libraries.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The term "dependency hell" has long been used to describe the nightmare of managing software libraries.
claims.rationale: The dated origin claim is specific but unsourced.

claims.claim_text: A 2019 University of Zurich study found 23% of open-source build failures were dependency-related.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? The Hidden Cost of Bad Setup
claims.anchor_text: In 2019, a study by researchers at the University of Zurich found that **23% of all build failures** in open-source projects were caused by dependency issues—not actual code bugs.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Dependency issues are a common cause of build failures in real projects, even when the application code itself is fine.
claims.rationale: I did not find an allowlisted primary source matching this exact year, institution, and percentage.

claims.claim_text: Before venv became standard, teams spent 4-6 hours per developer per month resolving environment conflicts.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? The Hidden Cost of Bad Setup
claims.anchor_text: Before venvs became standard, teams would spend an average of 4-6 hours per developer per month resolving environment conflicts.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Before project-isolation tools were widely standardized, environment conflicts could consume significant developer time.
claims.rationale: This quantified productivity-loss claim has no attributable source.

claims.claim_text: Python 3.12 is 5-10% faster than 3.11.
claims.anchor_text: **5-10% faster** than 3.11 - sounds small, but when you're training models for hours, it adds up
claims.suggested_rewrite: Includes performance improvements over earlier Python releases
claims.rationale: Python 3.12 has documented performance work, but this exact percentage is too broad and unsourced.

claims.claim_text: Python 3.12 improves error messages.
claims.rationale: The official Python 3.12 release notes explicitly document improved error suggestions and more precise f-string error reporting.

claims.claim_text: Python 3.12 improves the type system.
claims.rationale: The official release notes cover new type-parameter syntax and related typing ergonomics in Python 3.12.

claims.claim_text: Python 3.12's per-interpreter GIL is useful for parallel ML workloads.
claims.anchor_text: **Per-interpreter GIL** - experimental, but useful for parallel ML workloads
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Per-interpreter GIL** - a new Python 3.12 capability, though most beginners will not use it directly in this module
claims.rationale: Python documents the feature, but the module's workload-specific value judgment is not directly grounded.

claims.claim_text: PyTorch, TensorFlow, and Transformers all work perfectly on Python 3.12.
claims.anchor_text: **Full library support** - PyTorch, TensorFlow, Transformers all work perfectly
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Modern AI library support** - major Python AI packages support current CPython releases, though exact compatibility depends on the package and version
claims.rationale: This overstates compatibility and needs package-specific versioned evidence to stay precise.

claims.claim_text: Monterey, Ventura, and Sonoma ship with Python 3.9 by default.
claims.anchor_text: **Default macOS Python is 3.9** (Monterey/Ventura/Sonoma)
claims.suggested_rewrite: **The Python that ships with macOS is often older** than the version required for this curriculum
claims.rationale: The OS-version-specific default is brittle and unsourced.

claims.claim_text: Over 90% of professional Python projects use virtual environments, and global installs are malpractice.
claims.span_hint: section: Create Virtual Environment / Did You Know?
claims.anchor_text: Today, over 90% of professional Python projects use some form of virtual environment—it's considered malpractice to install packages to your system Python.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Virtual environments are standard practice in professional Python development, and installing project dependencies into system Python is usually discouraged.
claims.rationale: The percentage and normative claim are unsourced.

claims.claim_text: The venv module was added to Python's standard library in version 3.3.
claims.span_hint: section: Create Virtual Environment / Did You Know?
claims.rationale: The CPython 3.3 release notes explicitly list venv as a new standard-library module.

claims.claim_text: The full curriculum will cost about $3-5 in Claude API usage.
claims.anchor_text: **Claude API**: $3-5 total (most modules)
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Claude API**: costs vary with model choice and usage volume, so check current pricing before you budget
claims.rationale: This is a course-specific cost estimate with unsupported precision.

claims.claim_text: The full curriculum will cost about $5-10 in OpenAI API usage.
claims.anchor_text: **OpenAI API**: $5-10 total (if using gpt-5)
claims.suggested_rewrite: **OpenAI API**: costs vary with model choice and usage volume, so check current pricing before you budget
claims.rationale: This is a course-specific cost estimate with unsupported precision.

claims.claim_text: Even expensive GPT-5 requests cost about one cent each in typical use.
claims.anchor_text: Even expensive models like gpt-5 cost ~$0.01 per request for typical curriculum use.
claims.suggested_rewrite: For small prompts and short responses, many API calls cost only a small fraction of a dollar, but the exact amount depends on the model and token count.
claims.rationale: This depends on changing vendor prices and on unstated prompt/response sizes.

claims.claim_text: New Anthropic accounts often include $5-10 in free credits.
claims.anchor_text: Often includes $5-10 free credits for new accounts
claims.suggested_rewrite: Promotional credits and onboarding offers can change over time, so check the current console or pricing page.
claims.rationale: Promotional-credit claims are time-sensitive and unsourced here.

claims.claim_text: Together AI gives $25 in free credits.
claims.anchor_text: **Together AI** ($25 free credits)
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Together AI** (check the current billing page for any available credits or trial offers)
claims.rationale: This exact onboarding amount is time-sensitive and appears stale.

claims.claim_text: Together AI's free trial lasts weeks.
claims.anchor_text: Free trial lasts weeks
claims.suggested_rewrite: Trial availability and duration vary over time
claims.rationale: Trial duration is a mutable commercial detail and should not be stated precisely without a current source.

claims.claim_text: Hugging Face's inference offering gives access to thousands of models.
claims.rationale: Hugging Face's own cookbook describes serverless inference access to thousands of publicly accessible models.

claims.claim_text: GitHub secret scanning can alert partner providers when supported credentials are exposed in public repos.
claims.rationale: GitHub's documentation directly explains partner alerts for supported secrets in public repositories.

claims.claim_text: GitHub catches about 1,000 leaked keys per day.
claims.anchor_text: They catch ~1,000 leaked keys per day!
claims.suggested_rewrite: Large platforms and security tools catch many leaked secrets every day.
claims.rationale: This precise daily figure is unsourced in the module.

claims.claim_text: The .env pattern comes from the Twelve-Factor App methodology created by Heroku engineers in 2012.
claims.rationale: This is a real named methodology with an obvious primary source, but that host is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: GitGuardian detected 12.8 million hardcoded secrets in public GitHub commits in 2023, up 28% year over year.
claims.span_hint: section: API Key Security Statistics / Did You Know?
claims.rationale: This exact statistic is real and attributable, but the primary source is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Uber's 2016 credential-related breach affected 57 million users and led to $148 million in settlements.
claims.span_hint: section: API Key Security Statistics / Did You Know?
claims.rationale: This is a real incident with an available primary or near-primary source, but that source is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: 95% of Python projects use venv or virtualenv.
claims.anchor_text: **Today**: 95% of Python projects use venv or virtualenv
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Today**: virtual environments are standard practice across Python projects
claims.rationale: The exact percentage is unsourced and likely illustrative.

claims.claim_text: Stanford researchers estimate AI inference costs are dropping about 70% per year.
claims.span_hint: section: OpenAI API / Did You Know?
claims.anchor_text: Researchers at Stanford estimated that the cost of AI inference is dropping approximately **70% per year**.
claims.suggested_rewrite: AI inference costs have fallen sharply over time, though the exact pace depends on the model, hardware, and period you measure.
claims.rationale: The precise annual rate is unsourced here and highly sensitive to methodology.

claims.claim_text: Today's gpt-5-mini costs $0.00015 per 1,000 input tokens.
claims.span_hint: section: OpenAI API / Did You Know?
claims.anchor_text: Today's gpt-5-mini is even cheaper at $0.00015 per 1,000 input tokens.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Current API prices are much lower than early-generation LLM prices, but you should check the live pricing page for exact numbers.
claims.rationale: This is a dated exact price claim, which the schema rules explicitly disallow salvaging via a static citation.

further_reading.title: CPython venv docs
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary reference for how Python virtual environments work and what isolation they provide.

further_reading.title: Anthropic Pricing
further_reading.why_relevant: Useful for checking current Claude API costs instead of relying on stale course estimates.

notes: Code fences, quiz answers, and teaching analogies were intentionally excluded per pipeline rules. I focused on load-bearing prose claims with dated numbers, security guidance, named methodologies, or vendor-specific capabilities.
